text.title: Product Naming Strategies and Generation Process
text.summary: Seven strategies for generating product names, criteria for evaluating candidates, and a step-by-step generation process.

rule.statement: Apply all 7 strategies to keywords and generate at least 20 candidates before evaluating any.
rule.rationale: Single-strategy generation produces names that cluster; diversity requires parallel exploration across strategy types.

list.item: Descriptive — states what the product does (Dropbox, YouTube)
Invented — made-up word with no prior meaning (Spotify, Kodak)
Compound — two meaningful words combined (Facebook, Snapchat)
Metaphor — symbolic meaning borrowed from another domain (Amazon, Apple)
Portmanteau — syllables from two words blended (Pinterest = pin + interest, Instagram = instant + telegram)
Alliteration — repeated initial sound aids memory (PayPal, Coca-Cola)
Acronym — initialism for the product's full name (IBM, NASA)

rule.statement: Reject any candidate that fails more than one of these six tests: easy to spell, easy to pronounce, memorable, .com domain available, no trademark conflicts, works internationally.
rule.rationale: A name that fails on domain or trademark is unusable regardless of how memorable it is; a name with pronunciation ambiguity fails at word-of-mouth.

rule.statement: Target 5-10 characters. Avoid hyphens, numbers, and ambiguous letters (l/I/1, O/0).
rule.rationale: Short names attract premium pricing; very long names are unmemorable; ambiguous characters kill voice spelling.

section.title: Generation Process

rule.statement: Define brand attributes (3-5 adjectives) and keyword buckets (product, benefit, emotion) before generating any names.
rule.rationale: Names generated without a brief converge on generic register. Brief anchors diversity.

list.item: Step 2: List keywords by bucket — product, benefit, emotion
Step 3: Apply each strategy to each keyword bucket
Step 6: Test pronunciation with 5 people from target market

section.reference: Structured brief template for capturing attributes, keywords, candidates, and top 3.

antipattern.description: Prompting an LLM without explicit forbidden-words list produces names ending in -ly, -ify, -hub, or starting with Smart-, AI-.
antipattern.reason: LLMs are mode-collapsed on startup naming patterns. Same prompt yields Lify, Spark, Loop, Flux, Atlas, Nova, Pulse repeatedly. Diversity requires an anti-pattern list in the prompt.

antipattern.description: Accepting a name without native-speaker pronunciation test.
antipattern.reason: Agent-generated names like "Krapp", "Pschitt", or "Sega" (Italian: saw/handjob) have culturally negative meanings agents miss. Requires L1 speaker review.

example.note: Task Management Tool — complete generation run
example.code:
Attributes: Simple, fast, powerful Keywords: tasks, workflow, automation Anti-patterns: NO [-ly, -ify, -hub, AI-, smart-, Spark, Atlas, Nova]
Generated: TaskFlow (compound), Tasko (invented), QuickTask (descriptive), Flowdo (portmanteau), Taskr (invented) Filtered: TaskFlow (.com available, 8 chars, 2 syllables) Winner: TaskFlow
